title: A cell that is not a cell
testCase: Did the first row become a header row, with <th> instead of <td>?
blocks.0.text: The table works. The first row is "Model, Gears, Price" — the column names. You know it is a header row.
blocks.1.text: But look at the code. What says so? Nothing. "Model" is a <td>. "Black" is a <td>. To a machine the two are identical: a cell with text in it. The header row is a header only because it comes first and because you decided so.
blocks.2.text: The same thing again: the meaning is in your head, not in the code.
blocks.3.text: Why this is worse than it sounds
blocks.4.text: You read a table with your eyes. You see the whole grid at once. Your gaze jumps to the top, spots "Price", and comes back — in half a second, without thinking.
blocks.5.text: A blind visitor hears the table cell by cell. "Black. 21. 18 leva. Red. 7. 12 leva."
blocks.6.text: Twenty-one what? He heard "Gears" fifteen cells ago. He must have memorised it — and count in his head which cell he is on right now. With three columns it is painful. With eight it is impossible.
blocks.8.text: <th> instead of <td>. Table header instead of table data.
blocks.10.text: Now the reader says: "Gears: 21. Price: 18 leva." It pairs the column name with the cell all by itself. The person understands the table without memorising a thing.
blocks.11.text: And as a bonus: <th> comes out bold and centred all on its own. But that is a side effect, not the reason. If you use <th> for the look, you will get it wrong the day you want the heading left-aligned.
blocks.14.text: Headers are not only at the top. Sometimes the first COLUMN is the header — the model names. Then those are <th> too, one at the start of each row.
blocks.17.text: A large table has three parts, and each has a tag:
blocks.18.items.2: <tfoot> — the summary row at the bottom: totals, averages
blocks.20.text: On a small table they are optional. On a big one they are priceless: when you scroll a long table, the header row can stay stuck to the top — but only if you said which one it is. And when printing, it repeats on every page.
blocks.21.text: Your turn. Make the first row a header row. And if you like, add <thead> and <tbody>.
